---
title: "R Episode 1 — Introduction to R and the IDE"
---

::: {.callout-note}
## Objectives
By the end of this episode you will be able to:

- Run R interactively in RStudio (or Positron).
- Use R as a calculator and understand operator precedence.
- Assign values to variables and inspect them.
- Recognize and find R's data types.

**Teaching:** 30 min · **Exercises:** 15 min
:::

## R and the IDE

**R** is a language for data analysis and statistics. You'll write R inside an **IDE** — most
commonly **RStudio**, or **Positron**, Posit's next-generation IDE built on the VS Code engine.
Either works for this track; we'll show RStudio's four panes (Console, Source, Environment,
Plots/Help) but everything transfers.

::: {.callout-tip}
## Instructor note
Have everyone identify the **Console** vs the **Source** editor. New learners often type into the
wrong pane. Encourage writing in the Source editor and sending lines with Ctrl/Cmd+Enter — it's
the reproducible habit.
:::

## R as a calculator (live coding)

```r
1 + 100
3 + 5 * 2          # multiplication before addition
(3 + 5) * 2        # parentheses change precedence
2^10               # exponentiation
7 %% 3             # modulo (remainder) -> 1
```

Comparisons return logical values:

```r
1 == 1             # TRUE
1 != 2             # TRUE
3 > 2 & 2 > 1      # TRUE AND TRUE -> TRUE
```

## Variables and assignment

R's idiomatic assignment operator is `<-` (RStudio shortcut: Alt/Option + `-`):

```r
weight_kg <- 55
weight_kg                      # print it
weight_kg * 2.2                # convert to pounds
weight_lb <- weight_kg * 2.2   # store the result
```

Names are case-sensitive and should be descriptive. Prefer `snake_case`.

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Challenge 1 — Predict and check
What do these print? Predict, then run them.

```r
mass <- 47.5
age  <- 122
mass <- mass * 2.3
age  <- age - 20
mass > age
```

### Solution
`mass` becomes `109.25`, `age` becomes `102`, so `mass > age` is `TRUE`. Reassignment uses the
*current* value on the right-hand side before storing the new one.
:::

## Types

Every value has a type. Check with `class()`:

```r
class(3.14)        # "numeric"
class(1L)          # "integer"  (the L suffix)
class(TRUE)        # "logical"
class("forge")     # "character"
```

Knowing types matters because operations and functions behave differently per type — and
type mistakes are a top source of bugs.

::: {.callout-important}
## Key points
- Write code in the Source editor; send lines to the Console (Ctrl/Cmd+Enter).
- Use `<-` to assign; prefer descriptive `snake_case` names.
- Everything has a `class()`; types drive behavior.
:::

Next: [Episode 2 — Project Management & Reproducibility](r-02-project-management.qmd).
